content.full_text:
November 13, 1991




Frank C. Creasey, President
Mendocino County Associates, Inc.
P.O. Box 125
Mendocino, CA 95460

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice
                                            Our File No. I-91-494

Dear Mr. Creasey:

     You have requested information regarding the application of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ to your participation in a
legal action to remove a person from office. Because you have not
provided sufficient information concerning the action, we are
treating your request as one for general information.  (Regulation
18329(c)(4)(F).)²

     The Act does not prevent a nonprofit public benefit
corporation from participating in a legal action to remove a city
councilman from office. Under the Act, a nonprofit corporation is
treated as a "person"³ and is not precluded from participating in
the political process, including making contributions to
candidates and committees. There is nothing in the Act which
would prevent a nonprofit corporation from participating in an
action to remove an elected official from office.




_______________

1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
     are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
     regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
     18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
     Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
     immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
     (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3    See, e.g., Sections 82047 and 85102.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-494
Page 2


     You have not provided any information concerning the nature
of the legal action to be taken. You have only indicated that the
councilman against whom the action is to be taken is allegedly in
office illegally because of a failure to meet residency
requirements.

     Depending upon the nature of the action, it is possible that
the corporation will be making a contribution. For example, if
the legal action involves a recall election, payments toward the
recall election may constitute contributions to payments to
support a ballot measure. (Regulation 18215(a)(1).) Similarly,
if legal action is brought to challenge the results of an
election, payments toward the lawsuit may, in some cases,
constitute a contribution to support or oppose a candidate. (See,
In re Buchanan (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 14.)4

     Our advice is limited to provisions of the Act. We cannot
provide advice about other laws, such as state and federal tax
laws, which may restrict permissible expenditures by nonprofit
organizations.

     I hope that the above information is of some assistance. If
you have in questions regarding the above, you may contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By:  Margaret W. Ellison
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MWE:aa


_________________________________________________________________

4    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
